does: This paper conducts an interpretability study of multimodal large language models to uncover how they extract task-relevant visual information from noisy contexts. The authors introduce Retrieval Attention Mass (RAM), a token-level metric for measuring cross-modal retrieval, and identify a specialized subset of attention heads called Context-aware Retrieval (CoRe) heads. Through analysis across diverse visual domains and model scales, they demonstrate that CoRe heads function as dedicated information extractors that concentrate attention on query-relevant tokens, while other heads distribute attention more broadly. Causal interventions including ablation experiments show that removing only the top 5% of CoRe heads significantly degrades multimodal reasoning performance, whereas removing lower-ranked heads has minimal impact. These findings reveal functional sparsity as a structural property of MLLMs, where a small fraction of heads carry disproportionate responsibility for cross-modal retrieval.
connects: This work extends recent mechanistic interpretability research that probes the internal structure of large language models to understand their decision-making processes. It builds on prior work examining attention head specialization and functional division of labor within neural networks, adapting these insights to the multimodal setting. The paper responds to broader questions about how vision-language models reconcile heterogeneous modalities and extract relevant information from high-dimensional visual inputs. It engages with the interpretability literature that uses attention analysis and causal interventions to identify functional roles of model components, while directly addressing a gap in understanding vision-language reasoning that most interpretability work has focused on text-only models.
enables: This interpretability framework enables more efficient multimodal model design by identifying which components are truly necessary for cross-modal reasoning. Understanding that functional sparsity concentrates critical information retrieval in a small set of heads suggests opportunities for model compression, pruning, and acceleration without sacrificing performance. The RAM metric and CoRe head characterization provide tools for analyzing other multimodal architectures and comparing their information extraction strategies. These findings could inform future work on efficient vision-language models, interpretable multimodal training procedures, and targeted interventions to improve robustness and generalization. The work also opens questions about whether this sparse retrieval structure emerges universally across multimodal architectures or varies by model design choices.
